questions.scenario: DevPolaris has deployed a new revision of `devpolaris-orders-api` on ECS Fargate, but the replacement tasks fail health checks between 09:40 and 09:48 UTC. The runbook identifies `/ecs/devpolaris-orders-api` as the API log group and `/aws/lambda/devpolaris-receipt-email` as the unrelated receipt-job group. The engineer needs the first application startup error from the replacement tasks. Where should the engineer search first?

options.text: Start in the receipt Lambda group because its latest stream is newer than the failed ECS deployment.
Start with RDS engine logs because a database dependency can prevent an API from becoming ready.
In `/ecs/devpolaris-orders-api`, filtered to the incident time window and new task streams.
Start in the staging API group and compare the successful startup sequence before opening production streams.

questions.explanation:
The workload type and service name point to the ECS log group. The time window and new task streams keep the search small. Lambda logs may matter for side jobs, but they are not the API container's startup evidence.

Learn more in [CloudWatch log groups and streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/Working-with-log-groups-and-streams.html).

questions.scenario:
A checkout failure log is structured like this:

```text
level=error time=2026-05-02T09:42:18Z service=devpolaris-orders-api env=prod request_id=req_51 route=POST /v1/orders step=rds.insert_order error_name=ConnectionTimeout message=database connection timed out
```

Why is this better than `checkout failed`?

options.text: It identifies a database timeout, so the engineer can treat the database as the root cause without checking scope or frequency.
It provides a request ID and route, but the free-form message remains the only field suitable for filtering.
It provides enough request detail to replace metrics when estimating how many customers were affected.
It gives searchable fields that connect the request, route, failing step, and error category.

questions.explanation:
Structured fields make a future search precise. The log names the service, request, route, step, and error, so the next engineer knows where to inspect. It still needs metrics and traces to understand wider impact and timing.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs filter pattern syntax](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/FilterAndPatternSyntax.html).

questions.scenario:
A new `devpolaris-orders-api` revision repeatedly exits before it can pass its health check. The release engineer searches the task's CloudWatch log stream and finds this sequence:

```log
09:40:03 INFO release=2026-05-02.4 starting service
09:40:05 ERROR Cannot find module ./dist/server.js
09:40:07 INFO container exiting code=1
09:40:31 ERROR target unhealthy
09:41:02 ERROR service unavailable
09:41:31 ERROR service unavailable
```

Which event is the first useful error for diagnosing the failed revision?

options.text: `Cannot find module ./dist/server.js`, because it names the startup failure before repeated symptoms.
Start with `target unhealthy` because target health is the first production gate that failed externally.
Start with the repeated `service unavailable` entries because frequency is the strongest indicator of root cause.
Start with `container exiting code=1` because exit status is more actionable than the preceding module path.

questions.explanation:
The useful clue is the first specific failure that changes the next check. Missing `./dist/server.js` points toward image build, artifact path, or startup command. The later messages are symptoms after the app failed to start.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs Insights query syntax](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/CWL_QuerySyntax.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris stores high-volume JSON checkout logs in CloudWatch Logs. Engineers repeatedly query `request_id` and `order_id`, support needs 30 days of interactive investigation, older logs are retained only for audit, and the on-call team wants a metric when new events contain `error_name=PaymentDeclined`. Which three changes match those requirements without scanning or retaining more data than necessary?

options.text: Use narrow time ranges and the owned production log group for Logs Insights queries.
Keep all logs in the Standard class forever because archival requirements make retention policies unsafe.
Evaluate field indexes for frequently searched equality fields such as request ID or order ID.
Create a metric filter for the stable payment-declined pattern on incoming events.
Add customer email as a metric dimension so each declined payment has its own time series.

questions.explanation:
Focused groups and time ranges reduce scanned data, field indexes can improve common equality searches, and a metric filter can turn a stable incoming log pattern into an alarmable metric. Retention and log-class choices should separate interactive investigation from older audit needs instead of keeping everything in the most expensive shape. Customer identity is also unsuitable as a high-cardinality metric dimension.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs Insights query cost](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/AnalyzingLogData.html) and [metric filters](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/MonitoringLogData.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris routes ECS container logs for `devpolaris-orders-api` into `/ecs/devpolaris-orders-api`. During a failed release, the engineer sees streams `orders-api/orders-api/3b7c8f2a` and `orders-api/orders-api/91d4a011`, with a timestamped event reporting `request_id=req_7 step=rds.insert_order`. How should the engineer interpret the log group, streams, and event?

options.text: Treat each stream as one application request because the stream suffix changes between runtime sources.
The group is the home for related service logs, each stream is one runtime source, and the event is one timestamped message.
Treat the group as one ECS task and the streams as individual containers across every service deployment.
Search the whole group without stream or time filters first so older deployments remain part of the comparison.

questions.explanation:
CloudWatch Logs uses a simple hierarchy: group, stream, event. For ECS, streams often line up with task or container runtime sources. That model helps you narrow a deploy investigation to the task streams that were created around the failure.

Learn more in [CloudWatch log groups and streams](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/Working-with-log-groups-and-streams.html).

questions.scenario:
A receipt write failed after the order was accepted:

```text
level=error service=devpolaris-orders-api request_id=req_8X order_id=ord_8x7k2n route=POST /v1/orders step=s3.write_receipt bucket=devpolaris-orders-prod-objects key=receipts/2026/05/02/order_ord_8x7k2n/receipt.pdf error_name=AccessDenied
```

What is the best next move?

options.text: Inspect only the bucket policy because `AccessDenied` rules out identity policy and KMS causes.
Search by order ID but ignore the exact key until S3 confirms the bucket exists.
Check whether the ECS task restarted near the error before using the operation and resource fields.
Search by request ID and order ID, then inspect the S3 bucket, key, IAM role, and encryption path.

questions.explanation:
The log gives concrete evidence for the next investigation. It names the failing operation, request, order, bucket, key, and error type. That points to S3 access and configuration, not a blind ECS or RDS change.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs filter pattern syntax](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/FilterAndPatternSyntax.html).

questions.scenario:
Support reports that order `ord_9` was rejected as a duplicate. An engineer searches the production log group by request ID rather than reading every nearby error:

```log
09:42:16 INFO request_id=req_A order_id=ord_9 step=checkout.start
09:42:17 WARN request_id=req_A step=dynamodb.put_idempotency_key error_name=ConditionalCheckFailedException
09:42:17 ERROR request_id=req_A step=checkout.abort reason=duplicate_request
09:42:18 ERROR request_id=req_B step=rds.insert_order error_name=ConnectionTimeout
```

For `req_A`, what should the engineer investigate first?

options.text: The idempotency or duplicate-request path, because the first specific failure for `req_A` is DynamoDB conditional check failure.
Investigate the RDS timeout for `req_B` first because it occurred one second later in the same log group.
Treat the conditional check as DynamoDB capacity pressure before confirming whether the idempotency key already exists.
Retry `req_A` with a new idempotency key before checking whether the original request already completed.

questions.explanation:
Request IDs keep the search scoped. For `req_A`, the first useful error is the DynamoDB conditional check, which may be expected duplicate protection or a client retry issue. A different request's RDS timeout should not steer this investigation.

Learn more in [DynamoDB condition expressions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.ConditionExpressions.html).
